error.signature: AI says any International Driving Permit or home license lets you drive in Japan

verdict.summary: Japan only recognises IDPs issued under the 1949 Geneva Convention; permits under the 1968 Vienna Convention are invalid. Long-term residents must convert via gaimen kirikae at a prefectural licence centre, with proof of 3+ months' stay in the issuing country after the licence was obtained.

dead_ends.action: Drive on a 1968 Vienna Convention IDP (e.g. issued in Germany, China mainland, or Vietnam)
dead_ends.why_fails: Japan is party only to the 1949 Geneva Convention; Vienna-Convention IDPs confer no driving privilege, and driving on one is unlicensed driving. A small set of countries (e.g. Germany, France, Switzerland, Taiwan) instead use a special regime: home licence plus official Japanese translation.

dead_ends.action: Rely on a Geneva IDP indefinitely as a resident
dead_ends.why_fails: An IDP is valid at most one year from landing, and residents cannot renew it by briefly leaving and re-entering; long-term residents must convert to a Japanese licence (gaimen kirikae), which requires documents proving 3+ months of stay in the issuing country after obtaining the licence.

workarounds.action: Check your issuing country's regime first, then convert early via gaimen kirikae
workarounds.how: Geneva-Convention countries: use the IDP for up to 1 year. Special-regime countries: carry the home licence plus a JAF/embassy translation. Residents: book the prefectural licence centre conversion early - knowledge and practical tests apply to most nationalities, and rules tightened in October 2025.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: This canon is about licence validity; the other is about the physical hazard of left-hand traffic once you are legally driving.
